0:0.rendering: The vision of Obadiah. Thus says the Lord Yahweh concerning Edom: We have heard a report from Yahweh, and a messenger has been sent among the nations: "Arise, and let us rise up against her for battle."
0:0.note: The Hebrew word for "report" (shemu'ah) echoes the opening of Isaiah 53, linking Edom's judgment to the larger story of God's justice among the nations.

0:1.rendering: Look, I am making you small among the nations. You are utterly despised.
0:1.note: Obadiah opens with a divine oracle against Edom that echoes the ancient sibling rivalry between Jacob and Esau.

0:2.rendering: The pride of your heart has deceived you, you who live in the clefts of the rock, whose dwelling is high, who say in your heart, Who will bring me down to the ground?
0:2.note: The Hebrew word for "pride" (zadon) carries the sense of boiling arrogance that leads to self-deception, echoing the same term used for the rebels at Babel.

0:3.rendering: Though you build your nest high like the eagle, though you set your nest among the stars, from there I will bring you down, declares the LORD.
0:3.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "build your nest" (sim tasim) echoes the soaring imagery of eagles in ancient Near Eastern poetry while underscoring the futility of Edom's mountain strongholds.

0:4.rendering: If thieves came to you, or plunderers by night, how you have been destroyed. Would they not steal only what they wanted? If grape-gatherers came to you, would they not leave some gleanings?
0:4.note: The Hebrew word for "destroyed" echoes the name Edom itself, turning the nation's proud identity into a prophecy of total ruin.

0:5.rendering: How Esau has been searched out, his hidden treasures hunted down.
0:5.note: The Hebrew wordplay between "searched out" (niphas) and "hidden things" (nistarav) echoes the name Esau, whose root means "hairy" or "covered," turning his own identity into a picture of exposure.

0:6.rendering: All your allies have driven you to the border. Your friends have deceived you and prevailed against you. Those who ate your bread have set a trap under you. There is no understanding in it.
0:6.note: The phrase "those who ate your bread" echoes ancient Near Eastern covenant language, exposing how shared meals and treaties become betrayal traps.

0:7.rendering: On that day, declares the LORD, I will destroy the wise from Edom, and understanding from the mountain of Esau.
0:7.note: The Hebrew wordplay between "destroy" (abad) and "Edom" (edom) echoes the judgment theme of the book.

0:8.rendering: Your warriors, O Teman, will be terrified, and everyone in the hill country of Esau will be cut down in the slaughter.
0:8.note: The name Teman, meaning "south," echoes the ancient wisdom tradition tied to Edom, yet here it signals the total collapse of their famed military might.

0:9.rendering: Because of the violence done to your brother Jacob, shame will cover you and you will be cut off forever.
0:9.note: The word "violence" here echoes the same term used for the very first murder in Genesis 4, linking Edom's betrayal of kin to Cain's crime against Abel.

0:10.rendering: On the day you stood aloof, on the day strangers carried off his wealth, when foreigners entered his gates and cast lots for Jerusalem, you were like one of them.
0:10.note: The Hebrew word for "stood aloof" (amad) carries the double sense of standing still and standing against, exposing how neutrality in the face of a brother's plunder makes you complicit.

0:11.rendering: Do not gloat over your brother on the day of his misfortune, and do not rejoice over the people of Judah on the day of their ruin. Do not boast with your mouth on the day of distress.
0:11.note: The Hebrew word for "gloat" carries the idea of looking with malicious pleasure, echoing the same verb used when Edom's ancestors mocked Jacob's family centuries earlier.

0:12.rendering: You should not have entered the gate of my people on the day of their disaster. You should not have gloated over their calamity on the day of their disaster. You should not have looted their goods on the day of their disaster.
0:12.note: The triple "you should not have" hammers home Edom's betrayal, echoing the ancient Near Eastern covenant language of forbidden actions against kin.

0:13.rendering: Do not stand at the crossroads to cut off his fugitives, and do not hand over his survivors in the day of distress.
0:13.note: The Hebrew word for "crossroads" carries the image of a fork in the path where Edom could choose rescue or betrayal, echoing the ancient Near Eastern custom of blood kinship obligations now horrifically inverted.

0:14.rendering: For the day of the LORD is near upon all the nations. As you have done, it will be done to you; your dealings will return on your own head.
0:14.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "as you have done, it will be done to you" (gemul yashuv b'roshkha) echoes the ancient Near Eastern principle of poetic justice, showing how Obadiah weaves Edom's violence against Judah into God's universal judgment day.

0:15.rendering: For just as you drank on my holy mountain, so all the nations will drink continually. They will drink and gulp it down, and become as though they had never been.
0:15.note: The Hebrew word for "drink" echoes the cup of wrath poured out on Edom in verse 15, turning their own violence back on them in a scene of poetic justice.

0:16.rendering: But on Mount Zion there will be those who escape, and it will be holy. The house of Jacob will possess their own possessions.
0:16.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "possess" (yarash) echoes the original promise of the land, now restored after exile.

0:17.rendering: The house of Jacob will be a fire, the house of Joseph a flame, but the house of Esau will be stubble. They will set them ablaze and consume them, and there will be no survivor for the house of Esau, for the LORD has spoken.
0:17.note: This vivid reversal of Edom's predatory fire imagery from earlier in the book turns Jacob's descendants into the consuming blaze, echoing how the prophets often flip the very metaphors oppressors use against them.

0:18.rendering: The Negev will possess the hill country of Esau, and the Shephelah will possess the Philistines. They will also possess the fields of Ephraim and the fields of Samaria, and Benjamin will possess Gilead.
0:18.note: This verse reverses Edom's ancient land grab by promising Israel's tribes will reclaim and expand into neighboring territories, echoing the covenant promise of land inheritance.

0:19.rendering: The exiles of this army of Israelites will possess the land of the Canaanites as far as Zarephath, and the exiles of Jerusalem who are in Sepharad will possess the towns of the Negev.
0:19.note: This final verse of Obadiah uses the Hebrew word for "exiles" (galut) twice, echoing the earlier promise that Mount Zion's deliverers will possess Esau's mountains, now extending the restored community's reach to Phoenicia and beyond.

0:20.rendering: Deliverers will go up to Mount Zion to rule over the hill country of Esau, and the kingdom will belong to the LORD.
0:20.note: The Hebrew word for "deliverers" echoes the judges who rescued Israel in the book of Judges, pointing to a restored rule that finally belongs solely to YHWH.
